q: Sound is a sequence of pressure waves which propagates through compressible media such as air or water.
During their propagation, waves can be reflected, refracted or attenuated by the medium.
The followings are the properties of media which affect the sound propagation.
A relationship between density and pressure determines the speed of sound within the medium.
This relationship, affected by temperature, determines the speed of sound within the medium.
The viscosity of the medium determines the rate at which sound is amplified.
For many media, such as air or water, attenuation due to viscosity is negligible.
Speed of a sound wave can also be affected by the density, temperature, and elasticity of the medium through which the sound waves travel.
The speed of sound will be the same for different media.
In a solid medium, the speed of sound is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}$.
In the formula $v = \sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}$, $Y$ is Young's modulus.
In liquids, the velocity of sound is given by a similar expression, $v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$.
In the formula $v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$, $B$ is the bulk modulus.
In gases, the velocity of sound is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma p}{\rho}}$.
In the formula for velocity in gases, $\gamma$ is the adiabatic constant of gas.
Longitudinal sound waves travel faster in solids than they do in liquids than they do in gases.
Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are elasticity properties of the medium.
The SI units of Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are $\text{N m}^{-2}$.
The level of intensity of a sound is measured relative to a reference intensity value $I_o$.
The reference intensity value $I_o$ is taken as $10^{-10} \text{ W m}^{-2}$.
The reference intensity corresponds to the minimum intensity of sound that can be heard.
The minimum intensity of sound that can be heard is known as the threshold of hearing.
The decibel (dB) is the unit used for measuring sound intensity.
For every $10 \text{ dB}$ added to the sound level, the loudness of the sound is increased by $10$ times.
A $120 \text{ dB}$ sound will be $100$ times louder than a $100 \text{ dB}$ sound.
Sound intensity is a direct measure of the power reaching an eardrum measured in $\text{W m}^{-2}$.
Echoes are produced where a sound wave is reflected from a large and soft surface.
An echo is repetition of sound due to the reflection of sound.
Reflection of sound obeys the laws of reflection.
To reduce the effect of echo in buildings, walls are covered up with padding.
To reduce the effect of echo in buildings, the floors are covered with rugs and carpets.
Echo can also be used to determine the distance $d$ between a source of sound and the reflecting surface.
The formula used to determine distance using echo is $v = \frac{2d}{t}$.
Some animals such as bats are known to use echolocation to navigate and locate their prey.
Echolocation is the special ability of emitting sounds and interpreting the echoes.
A reverberation is a reflection of light or sound waves.
An example of a reverberation is the sound bouncing around in a large speaker.
Audible means able to be heard.
Audibility means the ability to be heard or capability of detecting sounds in certain range of frequencies.
The human's ear is capable of detecting sounds only in a certain range of frequencies.
The range of frequencies a person can hear is known as the range of audibility.
For humans with normal hearing, the lower limit is $200 \text{ Hz}$.
For humans with normal hearing, the upper limit is about $20\ 000 \text{ Hz}$.
These limits are known as the limit of audibility.
By using frequency generator, the audible frequency range can be determined.
Low frequency sounds which are under the lower limit of audibility are called infrasound.
Infrasound cannot be heard by human.
High frequency sounds which are above the upper limit of audibility are called ultrasound.
Ultrasound can be heard by human ear.
Bats and whales use ultrasound in echolocation.
Most sonar technologies use ultrasound.
Manufacturers of concrete use ultrasound to check for cracks or cavities.
Ultrasound can also be used to inspect metal pipes and measure the thickness of the wooden boards.
A prenatal ultrasound test uses high-frequency sound waves, inaudible to the human ear.
Ultrasonic scalers use ultrasound to remove calculus (plaque) deposits from the teeth.
The transmission of high energy ultrasound may result in the certain of cavitation bubbles.
Cavitation bubbles are created at sites of rarefaction.
The sonar is a type of technology that works based on echoes.
Infrasound waves have long wavelengths, and are thus able to travel over long distances.
The Doppler effect is the change in frequency of a wave related to an observer due to the relative motion between the source and observer.
The Doppler effect is observed only in sound waves and not in electromagnetic radiations.
The frequency of the siren heard by the observer is denoted by the symbol $f_o$.
When the source and observer are approaching relative to each other, the frequency heard by the observer is less than the frequency produced by the source.
The speed of sound in the medium is represented by the variable $v$.
The negative sign ($-v_s$) is used in the Doppler equation when the source moves towards the observer.
The positive sign ($+v_o$) is used in the Doppler equation when the observer moves towards the source.
When both the source and observer are stationary, the values of $v_o$ and $v_s$ are equal to zero.
Red shift occurs when the earth and a star are moving towards each other.
Radar is a form of echolocation that uses sound waves instead of electromagnetic waves.
Doppler ultrasound is a technique that can be used to examine blood flow in arteries.
The pitch of a note is determined by its loudness.
A greater frequency of a sound wave results in a higher pitch.
The loudness of a note depends upon the amplitude of the wave that produces it.
If the amplitude of a sound wave is doubled, the loudness is two times greater.
Quality or timbre distinguishes two sounds of the same loudness and same pitch emitted by two different instruments.
The timbre of a sound depends on its waveform, which varies with the number of overtones.
Intensity is defined as the energy transmitted per second per unit area held normally to the direction of sound.
The intensity of a wave is inversely proportional to the square of the amplitude of the wave.
Loudness is an objective quantity that does not depend on the observer.
For a particular volume of sound, some may find it loud, while others might find it soft.
The amplitude of air molecules for a sound at the threshold of hearing is a very large value.
The Doppler equation is represented as $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$.
Doppler ultrasound can provide information about the heartbeat of a fetus.
In the red shift phenomenon, the wavelength of light shifts towards the blue end of the spectrum when moving away.
Radar is used for applications like police radar and weather radar.
The energy transmitted per second per unit area is the definition of intensity.
The angular frequency of a sound wave is denoted by the symbol $\omega$.
Loudness depends on the subjective perception of the observer.
Sounds with the same frequency and pitch can still sound different due to their timbre.
The intensity of a sound wave is directly proportional to the square of the angular frequency $\omega$.
The Doppler effect cannot be used to examine organ functions like the kidney.
When a source recedes from an observer, the frequency heard is less than the source frequency.
The term $v_s$ represents the speed of the source.
The term $v_o$ represents the speed of the observer.
The waveform of a sound produced by a violin differs from that of a tuning fork.
High frequency sound is associated with lower pitch.
The Doppler effect is limited strictly to sound and electromagnetic radiation.
Frequency and pitch are unrelated concepts in acoustics.
The density of the medium is represented by $\rho$ in the intensity formula.
The Doppler equation $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$ uses $v$ to represent the speed of sound.
